got these to use at our annual seafood boil that includes corn on the cob.  They worked using regular size butter cubes, however, we did shorten each stick to make sure the pressure top pieces set inside the holder.  I recommend you leave them in the fridge until just before use... if you let them warm up to room temp, we found it kind of a mess.  If the butter were colder, it would have been easier to smooth onto the corn and to dispense using the top pressure lid.  I put them in the top rack of dishwasher after use and the cleaned up perfectly.  It would be a bit messier to wash by hand because of the grease aspect.  My family all voted on these and gave them the thumbs up.

I bought these to put on my granite counter top to try and mitigate some of the water off the granite... I put my soap dispenser bottle on one.. the dish soap on another and the other I put until the faucet handle.  I set them a little off the edge of the sink.  It is a perfect fix, but I think it helps some. The water drains away and into the sink - mostly.  I just think there is no real way to keep the water away from the granite completely.  They are a sturdy silicone or silicone like material.  They mostly stay put. They wash up easily and nicely. If you were using them strictly as made for - a soap dish - I can't imagine you would be happy with them.  I never hesitate to update my reviews should new info seem useful

Also, I sleep warm and and can’t stand a hot pillow under my head.  I thought the pillowcase would stay cooler as some had said it did for them.  I also didn’t notice it did anything for my hair or complexion.  So I haven’t used the second pillowcase I bought and won’t be getting any others.

Honestly, I've been trying to find a salt and pepper shaker set that 1) actually shakes salt and pepper out at a reasonable rate, 2) is each to refill, and 3) looks good. Mom likes to have exactly 2 mugs of coffee each morning and we have a single cup brewer - I wanted to make both her cups - one in the mug and one in the thermos keeping hot and she can serve it as she wants it. I have not used with cold drinks so I can't really remark on that.  It holds about 16 oz.  I do like how compact and easy it is.  I do recommend a bottle washer brush to help keep it clean - coffee is oily and sticks to stuff - including stainless.  It does go in the dishwasher too.

These CASAWARE 9 x 6 x 0.75-INCH TOASTER OVEN BAKING PANS (Silver Granite) are the perfect size for my excellent [[ASIN:B003Z34OME Oster Toaster Oven]], and they have a wonderful nonstick ceramic finish.  Cooked food spills slide right off the sheets under running water.  However, the pans are NOT dishwasher safe.  I put mine through the dishwasher anyway, expecting some damage to the nonstick finish over time.  To my surprise, the finish holds up well, but the pans themselves rust.  Water gets into the rolled edges of the pans, and then they drip rusty water onto my clean dishes as I take them out of the dishwasher.  The pans will rust even if you handwash them, as recommended by the manufacturer.  For me, the pans are a total loss--and a surprising loss, given how much these pans cost compared to other toaster oven pans.

Let me begin by saying I love Circulon cookware. My kitchen is a walking advertisement for it. It is designed for the cook, from the detailed handles that fit correctly in the palm of your hand to the way the pots clean up beautifully. I have never had a problem with the non-stick cookware peeling or pitting or scratching in anyway (unlike the Calphalon I have had in the past). Now specific to this set: This is the perfect housewarming or shower present for the bride to be. This set will last for years and will make even the most novice chef appear to be a professional on the first try out. There is every size in the set (perhaps with the exception of a really large stock pot) which a chef could ever need. Last weekend, I actually used almost the whole set simultaneously cooking an Italian dinner for my extended family, and I can assure you the set was well used, scraped and stirred for several courses. After the set was washed,  it looked like I just took it out of the box. Circulon may be a little more pricey than some lower cost manufacturers. But it's the old adage, you get what you pay for. Circulon is simply the best!

This kept overflowing from the top of the carafe, not because I put in too much water, but I believe, because the grinds kept getting past the filtration and stopped up the opening connection. My daughter wondered if it might be because I was putting the lid on too tightly for the coffee to pass through; however, if the lid is not locked down it simply won't fit beneath the filter. The first time it happened was my fault. I used a cone filter, rather than a large basket filter. I went out and bought the right kind, or at least what I think should be the right kind. The plastic basket is pretty large, and the paper filters do not reach to the top. But the problem continued to happen on occasion, and I could never figure out why. I decided to return it for a refund, although an exchange was offered. In fairness, I did purchase this from Amazon as a warehouse return or used, so maybe something was wrong. I could have tried another of the same, but I just ran out of patience. Even when it did brew correctly  -- as in coffee did not flow all over the pot and counter  -- I was really not that impressed with the taste. Given the price point and the specialty coffee endorsement,  I expected something more amazing. It is suppose to sprinkle the water over the grinds more evenly, but I could not taste the difference. I went back to my old coffee maker which has worked well and predictably over several years. I kind of think that the OXO brand is over rated and over priced, but perhaps, that's not a fair assessment. I could have tried a little harder on trouble shooting, and apparently, OXO will send replacement parts, but I'm tired and didn't want to work that hard. I just wanted something I could just take out of the box and make great coffee with that wasn't too complicated. This maker doesn't appear to be complicated, but it certainly has issues.

If you need  or like a real clock as opposed to your cell phone, as I do, this is THE perfect one.  Runs on two AA batteries... it's a sweep hand so it's COMPLETELY silent which is a big deal to me.  Plus there is a setting that back lights the face of the clock in the dark... just a little bit... enough to see if you wake up and want to know the time, but NOT enough to keep you awake because it's a beacon. It's a nice size... a lot of color options.. I chose the black case with the white face.  It's about 4"x4" just eyeballing it.  For me the perfect size to set on my night stand and I can see it when I don't have my contacts in.  Honestly, I've gone through a couple recently TRYING to find the perfect clock and this one is it for me.  Arrived quickly (I have prime) and in good condition. As always, if there are any issues, I will not hesitate to update my review.  Further I do NOT receive any compensation for my reviews. UPDATE:  2-15-2016  Just a quick update... Still LOVING this clock... I wanted to mention that the soft light that lights the face when it's dark is an amberish (soft yellow)... so it doesn't keep you awake like a bright white or blue hue.  Honestly, it's the best clock I've had in years. UPDATE: 3-31-2016... I purchased 2 more of these for other bedrooms.... they are just the best.  In size and function... in design. They run silently.  The are just bright enough at night, but not so bright they keep you up.  It's soft, amber glow - a color that doesn't disrupt sleep like bright white or blue hues...  I remain a devoted fan. UPDATE: 4-19-2016... Ok... I purchased yet another for travel... BUT... I accidently ordered the "black" face with white print... I do NOT think it is as easy to read as the white face with black print.... It's still the perfect, silent running clock - plenty noisy when you want it to be for an alarm... BUT I do not recommend the black face and white print.  Just a personal opinion...

Wow, what a clever idea! I always am making kabobs here in Florida, most of the time chicken but occasionally beef. There is nothing worse than taking the kabobs off the grill and bringing them into the kitchen and trying to remove the meat and vegetables from the skewers. Most times I burn myself trying to get a grip on the charcoaled metal. But if I am able to get a grip with a potholder and try to remove the food using a fork, I usually end up with broken pieces of food and some chicken/beef that will stay on the skewers in strings. When I saw these I was skeptical, but having used these once I can tell you that they are the neatest thing ever. Sliding down the skewer, all meat and vegetables are removed (in tact) and none of the food remained on the skewer. I am honestly surprised no one had this idea before. I think this would make a great supplemental bridal gift to give the bride who has everything! It is practical, functional, and bears the quality Cuisinart name. You cannot go wrong!

I was on a trip and discussing white elephant gifts with a friend and she told me about the time that everyone was literally "fighting" over this as one of the gifts. I ordered to try.  First let me say that I do NOT received free or discounted products to review. This bottle is relatively small.  But then, 4 oz, as it clearly states is NOT very big.  Be sure to read and compare with something you have in hand to be sure you understand sizing.  I included a pic to help with a realistic view of the size. The fragrance is mentholy... with a hint of citrus to me - not flowery... but that's just my opinion.  I like the smell.  The bottle says "essential oils" are used in making it and it is "Made in USA."  The bottle notes up to 200 uses.  You can be "refill" bottles.  But honestly this product is a bit overpriced for me. The first time I tried it, I used only 4 sprays (the bottle recommends 3-5) AND I forgot to "shake the bottle well" before each use. I found it didn't "tame" much of anything.  :( The second time I tried it I shook the bottle vigorously for a couple of seconds and used 5 sprays.  It worked to about 99% in my opinion.  Much better than the first try... I give them 3 stars for the product (mostly because the cost is soooo high) and added a star for a "made in the USA" product.  If they dropped the price to about $5-7 per bottle I would be a regular customer and might bump up to 5 stars.  A large canister of lysol is less than $5 and works about as well ... in my opinion. And lysol disinfects.. but it is not exactly the same product use set, I know. The product arrived quickly (I have prime) and in good condition. I never hesitate to update my reviews if new information could be helpful, including how many "uses" I actually can achieve.

This is a gorgeous little tea kettle and is much smaller than I expected. This kettle is only for heating up water and nothing else. Below are the PROS and CONS including some design flaws.  PROS - The unit is so pretty to me. - There are five temperature settings - 212 (best for black and pu-erh teas and tisanes such as rooibos and herbal teas), 195 (best for oolong teas and some herbal teas; can be used for yellow teas if left to cool slightly), 175 (best for white teas), and 160 (best for green teas although I brew these teas around 175).  The fifth setting is for 100 degrees to keep the water warm. - Cord storage is hidden underneath the unit. - The kettle fits nicely into the base as long as you line up the matching dots correctly. - I like the push button feature of the dial to turn on the unit and the blue light on the dial. - The lid on the carafe snaps nicely into place. - It reaches the desired temperature pretty fast. - Anywhere between 16 ounces and 48 ounces (2-8 cups) of water can be heated.  CONS: - There are no in-between temperature settings; it must be dialed into 212, 195, 175, and 160 for brewing or 100 for keeping the water warm. - There is no audible signal to tell you when the water has reached its desired temperature; the only way to tell is to watch the light go out. To me this is a design flaw because now I have to watch the water reach its temperature and there's no "automatic keep warm" feature. - The lid is difficult snap into the upright position when there's hot water in the carafe. - The neck of the carafe is so tiny that it is impossible to get a hand in the carafe to dry the inside unless your hand is small. I am able to get my hand inside when I am not using a towel; but as soon as I introduce a towel it's impossible for me. - The other design flaw is that when there are 16 ounces of water in the kettle, the unit feels hotter to me and it was almost impossible not to burn myself when pouring the water because of the steam and heat emanating from the top of the unit. Of course, wearing an oven mitt or holding a towel will prevent this possibility. - The lid is not removable. - At the current price of $79.99, I think this is too expensive a purchase for what you are getting.  It is important to read the manual for this unit. For instance, the carafe has to be seated correctly before turning on the unit or the power light will blink and the unit will not work.  This unit should not be washed because all components, including the carafe, are electric.  Only dry the inside and wipe down with a damp cloth.  Although this Capresso does the job it is supposed to do, the two design flaws that made me rate this 2 stars instead of 3 was that there was no audible signal to tell you when the water is heated to the desired temperature and the fact that the water is too hot to pour when boiling unless using a mitt or towel to hold the very hot carafe.  Other than this this is an average kettle.  I cannot vouch for its longevity as I've only heated water 4 times in this unit so far.  I will post an update if things change. If this unit were priced in the $29 range, I would have given it 3 stars despite its flaws.  If you are a real tea connoisseur, it is better to pay $170 more for the [[ASIN:B003LNOPSG Breville BTM800XL One-Touch Tea Maker]].  I personally purchased and own two of these.  If you only make teas with boiling water and want to make tea for company, then you might consider the [[ASIN:B007UEEJVK Nesco TM-1 Teamaker, 1-Liter]].  I also own this one and although it is average, at least you can make tea in it. I've attached my reviews to the other tea makers in the comment section of this review for those who are interested.  In summary, I would pass on this Capresso.  Update November 2013 - This kettle stopped working so now it's headed for the garbage. This is not the life expectancy I would expect and had I purchased it I would be very disappointed.

I have a Bissell Spot Bot and I purchased a Bissell 9400 ProHeat 2X Select Upright Deep Carpet Cleaner for my boyfriend; both are superb products!  However, I cannot phathom what Bissell was thinking when they produced this carpet cleaner.  I tried it on an area of carpet that was only slightly dirty; but the results were very disappointing.  What they got right: - Easy assembly - Sleek vacuum design - Useable power tools (power nozzle is good) - Easy carry handle - Excellent power cord holder - Nice power cord release mechanism (no need to unwind cord from power cord wrap because you can just release the latch to drop the cord to the floor) - Vacuum powers itself nicely along the carpet so there is hardly any pushing action required by the user  What they got terribly wrong: - The soft  PowerRoller doesn't clean; it's excellent on hardwood floors but that is not what this vacuum was designed for. - The carpet does not dry in 30 minutes as stated UNLESS you hardly use any solution.  Without using much solution, you might as well blow on the carpet with your mouth to try to dislodge the dirt because the results would probably be the same--which is no cleaning. - The solution tank leaks around the cap when filled appropriately.  I tried several times to reseal the cap but there was always some solution seepage. - The dirty water collector tank also has water seepage even if you don't go above the fill line. - I tried for 45 minutes to clean about a 6 x 7 area of carpet and it still looked as dirty as when I started.  I then used the power tool and some elbow grease and tried again which gave me better results but still not good enough.  My Bissell spot bot can clean the same area wonderfully but not this vacuum. - By the time the hot water reaches the carpet it is either lukewarm or too cold to be of any cleaning use.  In summary, if the Bissell didn't leak all over, this vacuum would be excellent (but perhaps overkill) for cleaning a wood floor because the cleaning pad buffs up things nicely (I tried it my wood floor).  However as a carpet cleaner, you would get better results by wetting a washcloth with warm water, applying some soap, getting down on your hands and knees and using some elbow grease to clean your carpet.

I ordered three different sizes of these glasses and I have to say I love them.  I am huge fan of double wall as it doesn't sweat.  You can use these glasses with hot or cold drinks and they will stay hotter or colder longer.  I have to admit I have not had this brand before so I was hesitant... I think there can be a big difference in quality... however, these arrived (all three size sets) well packed, completely intact and washed up perfectly in my dishwasher.  I have dropped one... on to a wood laminate floor... did not break. (probably a lucky drop but I was so glad)  Having owned this type of glass for years and years...This particular "style" with the dip in the middle sort of acts like a grip and makes them easier to hold onto... much nicer than my other brand which is a bit more awkward to hold and is narrow at the bottom which makes them less stable.... I do recommend careful use... put a bit of liquid in before adding ice cubes, just to be safe... and do be sure they are banging together in the dishwasher... Having said all of that... I have never (in 15 years) ever had one break with ice cubes, in the dishwasher or on even an occasional drop... but with a different brand.  Hopefully these will stand up just as well.  Having said that, I always update my reviews with any issues or useful info.

My 20 year mini blinds finally gave up and I found myself without curtains for my bedroom.  I purchased them as a temporary window cover and a really long tension rod.  In all honesty, I love them so much I may keep them up.  I purchased the white/silver - it's really a light beige with silver lines and dots.  They have a white felt like back which is supposed to be the "blackout" part - but if you're looking for a total block of all light - these aren't it.  A very faint amount of light does come through.  For me, that is actually perfect.  I don't want a full block of light.  The silver metallic touches are subtle and very pretty - reflecting light very softly - which to me, just adds to the elegance in my opinion.  The loops are a silver metal and easily slipped onto the extra, extra long tension rod I purchased.  Because the loops are metal, they slide really easily along the tension rod. If I could change ONE thing- I wish the metal loops were not quite as shiny as they are - more a brushed nickel look. The shiny silver metal is the only thing I don't love love - but not enough for me to change my mind on the purchase.  I love the look so much that I really think I will keep them even after my mini blinds (4-6 weeks out!) are installed.  In fact, I'm thinking about purchasing 2 more sets for the living and dining area.  I tossed them in the dryer on extra low for about 5 minutes and that smoothed out the slight wrinkles right out.  The material seems to resist wrinkles over all.  I just need a proper curtain rod and it will be a very lovely mini blind window cover.  I never hesitate to update my reviews should new info seem useful.

Before you buy this HOMESOURCE FLEX 'N SEEK CREVICE TOOL, be sure to check the shape of your vacuum's connectors.  If your vacuum has square connectors (at the extension wand, hose, or motor unit), then this tool will NOT fit.  It only fits vacuums with round connectors.  Thus it only fits one of my three Bissell vacuums.  The adapter adjusts for larger or smaller round connectors, but not for square connectors.  The crevice tool will fit into or under 1/2" wide spaces.  It won't fit under my refrigerator, as I'd hoped (though that was probably an unrealistic hope).  More significantly, the flexible tip isn't very flexible.  The tool is bent into a curved shape by the packaging, and I'm going to have to place weights on it to straighten it out.  Out of the box, it's hard to use, because the stiff curve makes it difficult to maneuver into tight spaces, and to keep the tool end flat against the floor for good suction.  Because this intriguing tool is poorly designed, and doesn't fit vacuums with square connectors, I rate it at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).

I selected this mini fridge with one idea in mind - keeping a carton of creamer cold in the office, away from the kitchen so I can always be sure to have creamer for my coffee when I am in the office. I have been sorely disappointed in this unit.  It has two functions, heat and cool. I have little use for the heating function but I have tinkered with it, and it seems to work reasonably well. I have not measured the temperature, but it does keep an open cup of coffee warmer for a longer period of time and it does a reasonable job of working as a cup warmer which is a nice thing if you don't like pouring hot coffee or tea into a cold cup.  The first issue I have with it - and it's a major one - is that it just doesn't get all that cold. It does well enough to give a canned or bottled drink a slight chill but nowhere close to that of a typical refrigerator. This is all right if you don't like drinks that are really cold, but that somewhat defeats the purpose. In this case, a freezer pack in an insulated bag works better for me at cooling a bottled drink down. It is enough to raise some concern in my mind about food safety if leaving anything of consequence in this little fridge for more than a couple of hours. I certainly would not trust an open jar of mayo that was kept in this fridge overnight.  The second problem is that this fridge is too small to accommodate a quart-sized carton of cream, totally defeating the purpose for which I got it. It almost fits, but not quite. This is probably my fault for not measuring, but it's unfortunate nonetheless.  I have real difficulty recommending this little fridge. It's better than nothing I suppose, but there are better options if you're willing to spend a little more.

I purchased 2 of these.  The bottle is a clear, ribbed glass - the pumper is plastic (tubing) and a brushed stainless (pumper).  I was nervous because I was going to use these with a pretty thick hand soap and the blue dish soap - which is also fairly viscous.  The opening of the bottle is big enough I did not need to use a funnel - which I initially tried - but the soap was too thick to make the funnel work.  After assembling the pumper to the bottle I cautiously pumped the top - they do need to be primed - but after about 6 priming pumps - the soap dispensed!  In both cases.  Victory!  I was so happy!  They both pump a very economical measure of soap - sometimes I pump twice to get a more hearty amount - but I think it will help in not wasting the hand soap and the dish soap.  They also come with cute wooden tags and a silicone coaster (white) to prevent slipping - which will happen once there is water around the bottle.  I chose not to use the wooden tags. I know which soap is which and I think the tags would quickly become kinda yucky.  But that's just my opinion.  They are not overly tall ( about 6 or 7" tall)or wide at the bottom (I'm thinking about 3 1/2 to 4" in circumference) - meaning they don't take up much space and look so much tidier.  Being glass, they are easily rinsed off.  I think if you really wanted to clean them in the dishwasher top rack you could - but they are easily enough washed by hand.  Overall, I am very pleased so far.  Having said that, I never hesitate to update my reviews should new info seem useful.

This PHILIPS AJH510/37 ALARM CLOCK is a decent night light--with four pretty, easily-changed colors to choose from--but not a particularly good choice for a bedside clock.  At the highest brightness setting, the digital time readout is dim, and it's hard to see what time it is in the daytime.  The night light (at the bottom of the clock) isn't exactly dim, but it doesn't provide much ambient light for the room--it serves mainly to show the clock's location.  Certainly this nightlight/clock will not keep any sleeper awake!  The clock won't win any beauty contests, either.  It's a mystery to me, why the manufacturer chose a battleship grey color for the fabric covering.  Perhaps to suggest metal mesh?  The grey background doesn't help the visibility of the dim orange readout at all.  And the grey color doesn't harmonize well with the faux wood plastic disk atop the clock.  The photo on the product page is fairly accurate regarding the clock's looks.  The clock's worst feature is the time and alarm-setting process.  Depending on what you're trying to do, you must press a button or a location on the faux wood disc, and then fumble around with the volume control wheel to change the hours and minutes, type of alarm (buzzer or nature sounds), display brightness, etc.  For me, the task of adjusting hours and minutes up and down, while holding the clock in hand and rolling a sideways-mounted wheel, is extremely awkward.  Also, I found the pictorial "quick start" instructions hard to interpret when figuring out the alarm- and feature-setting process.  The downloadable manual is similarly pictorial, but is slightly clearer.  At first, I was very confused by the "8UZ" setting, which was how I read the "BUZ" (for buzzer alarm) setting on the display.  As for the buzzer alarm itself, it is one of those that starts with a soft beep and increases in volume and frequency until the sleeper wakes and stops it.  (I like these.)  However, at the very highest volume, the buzzer is probably not loud enough to wake a very sound sleeper.  As for the preloaded nature-sounds (and three music sounds), I listened to them all, but didn't like any except the "train" (railroad boxcars rolling past)--which is only appropriate for go-to-sleep use.  (Go-to-sleep is one of the clock's features.)  The nature sounds were probably recorded from nature, but they sound artificial.  The music sounds include  an "angelic harp" that I would quickly tire of, for either wake-up or go-to-sleep purposes.  Because of the ugly-duckling appearance, awkward setting process, and dim clock display, I rate this Philips AJH510/37 Alarm Clock at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).

I bought one of these for the guest room and then went back and ordered another for my own bed, replacing a really mediocre mattress at a second home.  I own a ridiculously expensive Tempurpedic mattress with an adjustable base, so I had pretty tall expectations. I can say with great confidence that this mattress stands up to that comparison.  They arrived carefully shrink wrapped, for shipping.  They pop up very quickly after unwrapping.  NOTE: They are fairly heavy so I recommend two people to lift. Even the shipping box says "TEAM LIFT" on it.  It says 13" but mine is a bit more that that.... Typical of quality memory foam.  It would be nice if they sewed on some handles (like Tempur) for easier handling... but even that won't lower my rating...  The cover is soft and beautiful.  There is a very slight odor... but I am very sensitive and it didn't bother me. After one week, I don't notice any odor.  If you are not familiar with a memory foam mattress... it can be very firm or even very soft... I consider this mattress to be medium to medium firm.. of course this is MY personal opinion.  It properly softens and recovers for memory foam.  My husband and I agree that we toss and turn way less on a memory foam mattress than a regular mattress.  My husband sleeps a bit warmer than I do, but doesn't think that this mattress makes him too warm.  Another plus, this mattress is made in the USA.  The price point and quality to me is every bit as good as the stupidly high cost Tempur mattresses.  I always update my reviews if new information would be helpful.  Honestly, if you're considering a memory foam mattress, this one, at this price point is certainly worth consideration.  NOTE:  I am NOT compensated for my reviews in ANY way. UPDATE:  3/28/2016....  After a few weeks of use, I am still convinced these mattresses were one the best investments I've made.  Also remember that 25 YEAR warranty! Might be the last mattress I even have to buy.  Literally... and... just an FYI.... Memory foam mattress are NOT friendly to a whole host of critters, dust mites, bedbugs, etc...... in case that is one of your fears.  Again, the mattress is definitely not a "soft" mattress... most quality memory foam mattresses are medium firm at best.  Tempur does carry a softer, cushier mattress in their "cloud" line... but honestly for the CRAZY stupid price of the Tempur, I'll stick with this Brentwood mattress. You can purchase a more plush mattress cover if you're interested in a fluffier feel.... Even if you purchase an adjustable base from another vendor, you're still just a fraction of the price of Tempur queen size at about $5000.00 or more... depending on your line choice and base.....Thank goodness we have choices in the US of A !!!

This UTOPIA KITCHEN STAINLESS STEEL COMPOST BIN (for Kitchen Countertop;1.3 Gallon with Lid, Charcoal Filter, 1 Spare Charcoal Filter) is ideally sized for an indoor compost bin, and it has a tight-fitting lid.  However, the lid has large holes in it, and the charcoal filter inside the lid is see-through thin.  Thus the bin is a poor choice for my household, because we are constantly battling the ants that have colonized large portions of the Northern California coast.  The thin filter may contain odors--or may not--but it doesn’t present an adequate barrier to ants that are attracted by food scrap odors.  When the bin is lined with a compostable bag, the lid fits too snugly, and is hard to remove.  The charcoal filter is somewhat hard to pull out and replace, despite the seller's instructions to keep it free of food residue and to sun dry it weekly to avoid microbial growth.  I like the pail handle, and the bin size, and the all-stainless-steel construction.  However, this bin won’t work for me, because I want the bin to hold food scraps indoors overnight for later transfer to a big outdoor compost cart.

So these are "medium size" and will hold 2 eggs - traditionally you coddle one egg in a smaller cup, but this will work.  Remember "coddled" eggs are NOT poached.  They are delicious alternative to poaching or other water cooking.  Rub the inside of the coddler with generous amount of softened butter or olive oil.  Seal the lid top and place in boiling water pan (water below the lip of the coddler).  Cook per your desired temperature.  Around 10 minutes if you prefer a hard cooked egg - or around 5 if you like a more loose yolk - even less if you like a very runny yolk..  Scoop out with a small spoon or dip with toast sticks.  It's a real treat.  These coddlers are very nice because they are clear glass and  go right into the dishwasher. The metal clasps securely hold the lid on which is very important (you don't want water in the coddle) - and are easy to remove with a towel (they will be hot when they come out of the boiling water).  The only negative I will report is that the add clearly states it is a set of two.  I only got one.  Had a "replacement" order placed and again only got one. Once you’ve tried a good “lid-lifter” can opener, you won’t want to use a “lid-cutter” can opener ever again.  The “smooth edge” lid-lifters pull the entire lid off the can—the lid is held on with adhesive—leaving no sharp edges around the lid to cut you when the can is opened.  Unfortunately, this SWING-A-WAY SMOOTH EDGE CAN OPENER (522448; 7.5”, Black) is NOT one of the good ones.  It requires far too much hand strength to twist the knob and propel the opener around the can.  This is actually the first Swing-a-Way opener that I haven’t liked, and I’m really surprised that it’s so hard to use.  Two very good lid-lifter openers are the battery-operated [[ASIN:B00QHPOHSS Bartelli Soft Edge Can Opener]], and the manual [[ASIN:B000079XW2 OXO Smooth Edge Can Opener]].  My stroke-disabled husband (who is one-handed but still cooks) uses the Bartelli opener every day, because it opens cans reliably, without the need to twist any knobs.  This Swing-a-Way smooth edge opener might be a good substitute for one of those hand-squeeze devices that athletes use to strengthen their hand grip.

Let me preface this that I am now living alone and, the majority of the time, cooking for only one person. I hate heating up an entire oven to basically make a small dish of something (with the cost of natural gas in my area, it really is cheaper to send out sometimes than to be baking on a large scale). I also really don't like using microwaves unless when I really have something fast to warm up (I hate really cooking with microwaves as foods come out rubbery and unevenly heated).When I first saw the Black and Decker convection oven, I knew it was a name I could trust (having purchased B&D appliances in the past, most recently a food processor). I absolutely love this new oven and haven't used my full size oven in well over a month now. The size (20 x 17 x 12) is large enough for baking most casseroles. The oven heats up quickly and the food cooks evenly and thoroughly. Although I have only used the broiler once since I received this, I found again the cooking to be flawless and the cleanup a lot easier than I had anticipated. The unit is a good size for my countertop (not to big, not too small) and with the black and stainless steel body, blends in well with the rest of the appliances in my kitchen. I think this unit (at under $80) is perfect for apartment dwellers, singles, small families or senior citizens (think a great Christmas gift for your aging parents). I normally don't ramble on like this with most products in review, but I really love this countertop oven and I highly recommend this appliance.

This humidifier is ok for what it is. I used it in Montana and it seemed to be ok for a large room--small or medium would be more effective though. Now I'm in Arizona and I think it's even drier, and I don't think it works as well even in a smaller room, but I'm not 100% sure. It's a fine mid-range humidifier, I think.  Despite the "cool mist" advertisement it's not actually a misting humidifier. You're paying for a fan that sits over water.  It's easy to use, easy to clean, and I don't mind the noise. I prefer a fan sound at night so this does the job of my noisemaker when I use it.  I only have two complaints.  First, I'm annoyed at how expensive the filters have gotten. I used to be able to buy them at Target for like $8 (I've had this humidifier for years), but now I can't find them anywhere so I have to resort to Amazon where they're $12? Geesh. I've switched over to a different brand for filters (Fette Filter, filter A). To make the filters last longer, flip them over every time you add water (I usually did it every other day). I also put one of those Protec humidifier cleaning fish and it keeps the filter and tank clean for longer. Doing those two things, I would clean it about once a month while changing the filter when it got too crusty (maybe every two or three weeks?).  My second complaint is the blue light. It's BRIGHT. It's always on if the machine is on, no matter what. I have rolled up a hand towel and put it in front of the clear water gauge where the light is brightest, but because the machine is a light color it still makes the entire thing glow (and the wall or anything else around it!). I like a dark room at night, and sometimes I just don't have the determination to ignore the glowing blue light in my room so I don't always turn it on.  I've now switched to a BAYKA humidifier that really is a cool mist humidifier and it lets me turn the light completely off while running it. On top of that, I can put essential oils in a sponge ring where the air comes out, though that part isn't that effective like a real oil diffuser. I still like it more than this fan humidifier.

To only make grilled cheese sandwiches and panini, I thought this GEORGE FOREMAN 4-SERVING REMOVABLE PLATE GRILL AND PANINI PRESS (Black, GRP1060B) would be satisfactory for my needs.  Wrong, wrong, wrong!  My grill arrived new in the box, and it didn’t smoke or smell bad when I followed the manufacturer’s instructions to start by heating it empty for 15 minutes (to burn off any manufacturing oil or residue).  However, the budget-priced (about $29 on Amazon) grill is rather scary to use.  First, there is NO on/off switch or temperature control.  The grill heats when you plug it in, and it gets VERY hot in about 5 minutes.  You turn the grill off by unplugging it, and it takes quite a while to cool enough for you to remove the dishwasher-safe plates.  At least the cord doesn’t heat up, and it doesn’t spark when you unplug it.  Second, the hinge, which “adjusts” to handle thicker or thinner items placed on the grill, is very clunky.  When you open the lid and pull up, the hinge pulls up to make a wider space between the two grill plates. However, the hinge doesn’t pull evenly or all that easily.  Third, all of the black plastic parts (including the adjustable hinge) get hot when the grill is heated.  You really need to handle this grill with oven mitts or hot pads.  Fourth, the electrical cord is extremely short--shorter than most “short cord” countertop appliances that use a short cord as a safety feature.  Because the cord is so short, the cord won’t reach my kitchen countertop outlet unless I place the grill sideways on the countertop.  This makes it awkward to load the grill, which is really designed to be opened from the front.  The worst of these four bad features is the lack of a temperature control.  The only temperature is “very hot”, so it’s hard to toast grilled cheese sandwiches or panini sandwiches without burning them.  I wouldn’t dream of purchasing this grill for use in cooking meats or grilling vegetables.  While the grill isn’t exactly flimsy, it is clear that the manufacturer has put the minimum amount of material into the grill’s manufacture.  I’m pretty sure that the grill will burn out or quit working fairy soon if it is used at all regularly.

UPDATE 3/12/18:  After several months of uneventful operation, my humidifier began intermittently leaking badly onto the floor.  I finally figured out that the water reservoir in the base, which hydrates the wick, was overfilling.  This was happening because the valve on the refillable water tank, which controls the automatic delivery of water as needed during operation, was malfunctioning.  This was NOT user error.  It is a basic design or manufacturing flaw.  Rating changed from 5 stars to 1 star.  This VORNADO ENERGY SMART EVAPORATIVE HUMIDIFIER (EVDC300, with Automatic Shut-off, 1 Gallon Capacity, LED Display) has all the features that I want in a medium-room-size humidifier.  First, when it's running in the “Energy Smart” mode (indicated by a green light next to the lighted power button), it turns off automatically when it runs out of water.  Second, it remembers the settings when it's turned off.  After you power it off and on again while refilling the tank, you don't have to punch all the buttons again for (1) humidity level, (2) fan speed, and (3) automatic, continuous, or Energy Smart mode.  Third, it has a 1-gallon tank that lasts about 30 hours (when I keep it in Energy Smart mode, on high fan speed, with 60% humidity).  The tank is easy to refill, because it has a wide circular top opening, and isn't too heavy when filled with water.  The tank has a built-in handle that makes it easy to carry.  Fourth, it has a single wick (a mesh sheet that soaks up water from the tank).  This needs to be replaced when it gets hard enough that it won’t wick up water, or when it develops algae or mold.  The wick contains an antimicrobial additive, and you can extend the wick’s life by adding a bacteriostatic solution that suppresses algae growth to the water tank.  The wick is supposed to last 4-8 weeks; using the algae suppressant, my wicks last at least 12 weeks (in Energy Smart mode).  (This model uses [[ASIN:B01J664VSA Vornado EVDC300 wicks (2-pack)]].)  Fifth, the fan turns off when the humidifier sensor determines that the set humidity level has been reached.  You can set the humidity at anything from 40 to 60 percent (in 5-point steps).  The sensor is an approximation, of course--when my humidifier reads 60%, two other nearby sensors read 46% and 56%.  It takes a few days for the room humidity to reach the desired level.  (The fan can be manually set to run continuously at extra-high speed, if you want to raise the humidity level fast.  If you do this, check the water tank frequently, because with this setting the humidifier won't turn off automatically when it’s out of water.)  Finally, the humidifier has a water tank light that pulses a warning when the water level is low.

Up to this point, my main method for slicing blocks of cheese has been a large, stainless steel chef’s knife. It does the job but it’s also a bit awkward and admittedly not ideal. I looked to this cheese slicer as a possible replacement. I would politely describe my experience with it as “disappointing”.  This is a guillotine-style slicer – it has a handle with a thin steel wire that is used to cut through the block when the handle is pressed down, making a clean, straight cut. The surface on which the cheese sits is a nice marble slab with a groove cut in it so that the wire can pass completely through the block without leaving an uncut lip.  In theory this works, and I have seen it work well in other similar slicers. However, for this particular slicer the wire is so flimsy and weak that it bends when slicing and I believe it’s a matter of time (not very long) before it breaks and I have to restring it.  I can’t recommend this slicer. I’ve gone back to using my chef’s knife while I search for better wire to replace the wire that came in this slicer. There are less expensive options that work better.

Needless to say, having a relatively mid-sized powerful fan that can be battery powered, can be a life saver if we lose power for one or more days. My only concern with this is the need for 8 batteries, but I purchased a 24 pack and will have it handy. Ordered this tapestry because we wanted a world map for our living room.  The entire image they used has been squished width-wise and overly stretched length-wise to preposterous proportions. They should have rotated the image to fit it properly onto the tapestry but instead they scaled the image too narrow horizontally to fit and possibly stretched it out vertically - in the process they destroyed all continents and made much of the labels unreadable and pixellated.  The only nice thing I can say is that the tapestry material was thick and satiny with edges folded over for good border support. NOTE:  Amazon is combining reviews for several different types of aprons, so please be aware that this review is for the cotton denim apron being sold.  I have several aprons but I wanted one that came below my knees.  This cotton denim apron fits that bill. I love how it protects the front of my clothing when cooking or cleaning. The apron pockets are chest high in front; I usually prefer apron pockets a little bit lower in the front so I am unlikely to use ones that are so high.  It washes well and any shrinkage is imperceptible to me. Also, it barely wrinkles after coming out of the dryer and the little that it does wrinkle is not visible after cinching it back around the waist. The only serious wrinkling is the folding of the straps; those will have to be ironed to sit flat. I chose not to iron mine.  The BIG NEGATIVE for me about this apron is the design.  There is only one long strap holding the apron on.  There's a channel on each side of the chest area through which the one strap is looped through like an upside down U going from one side of the body to the other. As a result, to enlarge the neck strap, I just have to pull up on the strap and that reduces the tying ability of the remaining length along the back. The strap is long enough so that does not matter, but what matters is that any movement after it's tied has a strong potential to pull down in the neck area.  After wearing this apron for a while, I absolutely hate it because the strap around the neck which adjusts with how you tie it in the back, causes me to have a neck ache EVERY SINGLE TIME I USE THIS. It is only for this reason that I rated this 2 stars. If there were four straps (one on either side of the neck and one on either side of the back), this apron would have received 4 stars from me. It would have received 5 stars if they also put the pockets lower. This apron as it stands now is not usable to me.

I wondered whether this TRIPLE SCOOP 1.5 QUART ICE CREAM MAKER (TS-562-Red; about $59 on Amazon) would be an improvement over my much-used [[ASIN:B003KYSLMW Cuisinart 1.5 Quart Ice Cream Maker]] (about $48 on Amazon).  The answer is "Yes and No", but mostly "No".  +++Yes, the Triple Scoop is quieter (a lot quieter); it takes up less storage space (freezer tub and motorized lid only; no separate motor base and big plastic cover); and it freezes the ice cream slightly harder (at least when you use the Triple Scoop ice cream mix (one vanilla, one strawberry, one chocolate packet included).  +++No, the Triple Scoop has a too-small, too-narrow spout for adding ice cream mix (the mix spills unless you're very careful or you use a funnel); the finished ice cream is difficult to remove from the paddle (most of the ice cream comes out with the paddle, too); the Triple Scoop motor element is difficult to remove from the clear plastic lid for cleaning (the motor element plus lid is also hard to grip when you're attaching it to the freezer tub); the Triple Scoop has no recipes for making ice cream from scratch with your own ingredients (the instructions say to buy the Triple Scoop ice cream mix packages).  The two machines make about the same quantity of "soft-serve" ice cream (put it in the freezer if you want more solidly frozen ice cream).  Both machines use freezer tubs that must be placed in a freezer for 24 hours for best results.  (Note that if your ice cream isn't freezing, you can't just run the machine longer than the recommended 20 minutes or so, because the tubs warm up during use.)  The freezer tubs are hand-washable only (because they contain something liquid that freezes hard in the freezer).  None of the Triple Scoop parts are dishwasher safe; some of the Cuisinart parts are dishwasher safe.  The notion that you need to purchase Triple Scoop ice cream mix to make homemade ice cream is kind of ridiculous.  All you need for ice cream is 2 cups of milk, half-and-half, or heavy cream, plus some sugar and flavorings.  You can use recipes from anywhere, as long as you don't overload the motor by adding two many extra ingredients like chopped fruit or nuts.  I tried the included Triple Scoop Strawberry ice cream mix, and didn't care for it.  It tasted like strawberries, but also faintly like artificial flavoring and preservatives (so it's not for anyone who wants to avoid the chemical additives in commercial ice cream).  Overall, I much prefer my very noisy, storage-space-hogging, Cuisinart ice cream maker (shown with a purple tub from an all-purple model).

I purchased these for multiple reasons. The main reason was that I was moving. I was moving bc my apt kept flooding.  Luckily having been through other floods I generally store all my stuff in plastic containers anyways. Sadly a couple of my vintage Singer’s in wooden boxes had been left out during the last move & the last flood got them moldy & ruined them.  I am a bibliophile & these bags held not only huge stacks of my hardcover books, but my 40 pound Singer sewing machines. I also carried hundreds of pounds of yarn (50 per bag) and thousands of pounds of quilting fabric during my move.  It held up to paints, plants, garden pits, & pretty much anything I threw at it. I even put my pots & pans in these bags.  While I still thought they were a bit pricey I did buy some on sale & I think I bought 18 total that I was able to pack & take to my new place & unload & go pack again. Tho I now intend to leave some of my yarn in them as it’s waterproof.  Tho I intend to place it on a top shelf just to be on the safe side. Lol. Not one bag ripped, tore, or had an issue so far that I am aware of tho I have not checked them over very well. And I had no issues with the zippers tho I am positive that I packed several bags well over the weight limit on accident bc I was trying to keep certain quilting fabrics together for a quilt project.  I’ve bought some real duds on Amazon, but this was not one of them. This has by far been one of my better purchases & I’ve told my kids that are planning to move to buy the bags too bc it is just so much easier imo.  I literally used a taxi to move half my stuff (disabled & don’t drive) & was able to fold the bags back down to how they arrived after unpacking to take them back to be packed again.  I also made little tags and used color coded handkerchiefs I hung on the handles so I knew which room the bags went in or knew what was in them without having to open them in the wrong room once I moved.  It saved time.  I live in an apt & these bags take up a lot less space than the massive sterling & Rubbermaid hard sided plastic totes I normally use for moving & craft storage.  I still have them & use them, but storing them is a challenge.  Also, the bags fit under my zinus/spa sensations type elevated platform bed. It’s a frame amazon sells & you buy your own mattress. Normally I can fit 8 hard sided tubs under my bed (queen bed), but I’m limited by the height of the tubs. The bags fit or I’m able to squish to fit if I overfill them which is nice.  Depending on how full you pack them you can get 4-8. They are different sizes than my plastic tubs tho.  I only wish I had stumbled across these bags years ago given how many times I’ve moved. Lol.

UPDATE BELOW PLEASE READ UPDATE...I'll be honest.  I am sick and tired of reading reviews about exploding glass fronts, etc on toaster ovens.  I had a breville for over 10 years but the new ones have scary reviews.  So after months research and reading reviews I settled on this one.  So far, so good.  It's huge and will roast a whole chicken.  I have tested the temperature with multiple devices and it is nearly perfect with a degree or two - better than any other toaster oven I have owned.  The convection option is noisy - but appears to work.  It makes sort of odd sounds when it's cooling down - reminds me of the sounds that my ice maker makes when it's thinking about making ice.  Odd - but works.  So far it is easy to clean.  Learning the controls takes a minute or two, but once you get the hang of it - it's ok.  I am hopeful that it will work for a long time.  Having said all of that I never hesitate to update my reviews should new info seem useful. UPDATE 7/6/2021 I have been using this toaster oven for several weeks and overall I am happy.  If I could say one thing in the "eh" column it would be the timer bell.  It needs to be WAAAAY louder.  But if that's the worst I can say - it's still a good deal.  I will continue to update my review if new info would be useful. UPDATE 11/1/2021  I am devastated to write this update. The "toaster" oven no longer toasts.  The bottom heating units are dead as a door nail and this thing is only 5 months old.  It's junk.  So, I CAN toast if I want to catch the cycle half way through and turn it.  And before anyone asks - NO I am not using the "bagel" cycle!  It's just a huge disappointment. You'd really like to trust the brand but I guess as with most things - it has really failed.  I am so disappointed to have to shop for a new toaster/oven.  Can't recommend this oven for any one unless you quite literally have money to burn.

The VORNADO VS-410 ESSENTIAL FABRIC STEAMER comes with a big lint brush, a fabric brush attachment, a travel case, and an 8-foot cord with an attached Velcro strap (for securing the wrapped-up cord).  The steamer heats up quickly (in about 2 minutes); it delivers a fairly substantial amount of steam; and it stays cool on the bottom (so it won't burn or set fire to any flat surface you set it on when it's hot).  However, these good features are far outweighed by the many bad features of this product:  *** The steamer is designed only for steaming items that are hanging up--it strongly prefers to be operated in a vertical position.  If you want to steam an item laid flat on a bed, and hold the steamer horizontally (like an iron), the steamer spits water droplets even when not overfilled.  *** The water tank capacity is inadequate.  On a "max fill", my steamer delivered steam for only 8 minutes (not the promised 10 minutes).  Worse, when you open the top to refill the tank, there is still some steam inside, even after the steamer has stopped delivering steam and has gone quiet.  (The instructions do say that you should turn the steamer off and let it cool completely before refilling the tank.)  *** The water tank fill window is badly placed.  It's difficult to fill the steamer exactly to the "max fill" line, because you can't easily see the window while you're filling the tank at a faucet.  Worse, you cannot check the water level when you're actually steaming a garment without aiming the steam directly at your face!  *** The power button is badly placed.  Located on the top of the handle, the power button turns off at the slightest touch.  My steamer kept turning off when I was simply trying to steam out creases, until I realized that I was hitting the power button as I moved the hand holding the steamer.  *** For a travel steamer, this one is kind of heavy.  The steamer and fabric brush attachment alone weigh 1 lb. 11.5 oz.; add the lint brush and travel bag, and the whole thing weighs 1 lb. 15 oz. (almost 2 lbs.).  *** The power does not shut off automatically when the water tank is empty.  *** The 8-foot cord is just barely long enough for a steamer intended to be used on hanging garments.  *** The steamer needs cleaning every 3-6 months with Vornado CleanCycle crystals (two packets supplied with steamer).  My plan was to use this steamer for blocking hand-knit garments, which need to be steamed to give them a finished look.  However, it's a really bad choice for knitters!  The steam output time is very short, and the steamer doesn't work well in a horizontal position.  My old [[ASIN:B00005OU01 Sunbeam Garment Steamer]] (weighing 1 lb. 5.1 oz. complete with brushes and travel case) is easier to use, and includes the ability to deliver extra steam; it's a better all-around fabric steamer (but apparently discontinued by Sunbeam).  This Vornado steamer was a real disappointment, because I'm very happy with my other Vornado products (an oscillating fan, a room air circulator, and a humidifier).  I rate this steamer at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).

Update 1 month after purchase: I hadn't used it in a few days because the MOTOR is too loud so I was intending to move it across the room to see if that would help. When I woke up this morning, the humidifier was sitting in a bit puddle of water. When I carried it over to the sink and dumped it out and dried it off with a towel, I could see it was leaking more water out of a hole (or holes) in the bottom of the machine. What's the deal with that? And why did it just start leaking now? It's just dumb luck it didn't get anything else wet besides the metal table it was sitting on. Lucky for me it's within the return window, so I'm returning the humidifier. ---------------- I haven't had this humidifier for very long and so far it's OK. It puts out a lot of mist which is nice. I like how I can turn the blue light completely off, unlike most humidifiers. And I like how I don't have to buy or deal with filters.  The essential oils part--which is a big selling point--is a little useless. I've put up to 5 drops of oils on the sponge ring (the instructions recommend 3 drops) and I still can't detect any oils in the mist. Maybe if I stand right in front of it and blast myself in the face, but even then it's hardly potent enough to smell my oils. Can't say I'm impressed about that.  The only other complaint I have is that the power "button" doesn't work that well. It's less a button and more just a sensor like a touch screen. A poorly performing touch screen. It takes a lot of tries to get it to work so that's pretty annoying.

I recently sold my house and moved into an apartment where I intend to stay for about three months until my new house is ready. I love to grill in my old house and new house I had And will use a gas grill.  I knew living in a strange place for a few months and being out of my regular routine, I would really miss the healthier meals that I would make. When I saw this Krupp’s grill, I was a little hesitant.  Currently living in an apartment, I didn’t want to have to worry about smoke detectors going off, or anything else that might impact the building on them. From a personal perspective, I was worried that I wouldn’t like the taste of the food given that I’m used to growing on outdoor grills. Boy, but what a mistake I made! This is an absolutely great grill. I found very little smoke and cooking hamburgers, and they tasted great. I even think they cooked more evenly then they would’ve on the grill.  My only complaint, and that’s why am removing one star, is the cleanup. I did line the pan with aluminum foil which did help. However the inside of the grill underneath the heating element. It’s very difficult to clean. With the splashing of grease from the burgers it did take me a while to try to get this clean, and I’m still not sure if it was 100% clean. I probably won’t be using this quite as often as I would have an outside grill, but in a pinch, this is a great grill to use indoors for an apartment or a home.

So far, if I could rate these 10 stars I would.  This set is amazing.  I purchased them because honestly, I am tired of buying non-stick cookware - it lasts a year or so and then I am buying another.  Off gassing/ or worse..?  Who knows.  I decided that I would give up a little ease in cleaning for a healthier, more sturdy cookware.  I am thinking this may be the last set I will ever buy.  I read up and watched a few helpful videos on what to do, how to cook with stainless, how to clean, etc.  It is a DIFFERENT way all around to use stainless.  I highly recommend that you educate yourself while you're waiting for your set to arrive.  I have not had a single thing stick to my pots/pans - but I follow the "rules" of stainless use carefully.  One of the most important things to do is to heat your stainless BEFORE adding oil.  Make certain your pan is heated to the right temp - if you spritz a few drops of water and it rolls around - you're ready.  This set is heavy, sturdy.  But not too heavy - I will note that if you're sensitive to weight - you may find this set too heavy.  I love the glass lids.  Easy to see and they go in the dishwasher easily.  I do not recommend dish washering pans - its not hard to hand clean them.  There are also tutorials on doing that too - very useful.  I couldn't be happier with my investment in buying these pans. They are wonderful to cook with - the rolled edges allowing pouring of liquids without drips - every time.  I truly can't think of a single thing that is not good about them.  I would be thrilled if this was the last set of cookware I ever had to buy.  I looked at way more expensive sets, and cheaper sets - I settled on this one for personal choice reasons - one being the glass lids.  Having said all of that, I never hesitate to update my reviews if new info would be useful.

I love the fact that the three of them slide to fit perfectly together and, unlike other reviewers, I really think the idea of having only one plug is an excellent idea (it saves space and there aren't a bunch of cords hanging around my dining room). I've had this vacuum for 3 months, and got it off of QVC with more attachments than on here, but I never use the microfiber mop anyway. I'll put brackets around reviews for the attachments that don't apply to this amazon package but for someone who was wondering about the extra pieces (which you can see here on the QVC website [...]  This vacuum sucks a lot of stuff out of our carpet. With a dog and two cats we have a lot of pet hair around, and every time I'm done vacuuming the canister is full of dirt and hair.  The cord isn't a long or short length. I use a vacuum extension cord anyway, but if I don't then I need to change the outlet once or twice while vacuuming my main floor.  [I use the bare floor attachment for my kitchen. It works okay, but when you run it parallel to the wall, it won't get any dirt or crumbs against the baseboard. Instead, you have run it at a perpendicular angle, so that's a little annoying since I have to often run over the same floor twice in two different directions just to make sure I get all the dirt/crumbs around the edges, and it forces you to slowly make your way down the wall a foot at a time, only as wide as the nozzle. It's irksome to say the least. I think I'll just start sweeping before I vacuum, which is pretty annoying.]  [I haven't used the dry dust mop attachment (microfiber pad) because I haven't had any real use for it when I have the bare floor attachment. The kitchen is high-traffic area and for the amount of crumbs from food and dog food that land on the floor, I'd rather use the bare floor piece rather than the dust mop. If I had a wood floor without as much traffic I might use it, but the rest of my house is carpeted!]  The regular "motorized floor brush nozzle" works well. I have to cut out the hair from the spinning brushes every few uses--it's amazing how much of my own long hair ends up on the carpet (gross, I know). I don't think it gets the edges of my carpet very well either, but I didn't seriously expect it to.  I wish the turbo brush could have somehow been motorized, but it wasn't with my last vacuum either so I didn't really expect it to be. I use it on my carpeted stairs, and it works ok but could definitely work better and more efficiently if it was motorized.  The vacuum is very tippy, [especially with the bare floor attachment. It almost can't stand up on its own with that attachment], so I always lean it up against a wall anytime I need to stop vacuuming (that goes for both floor attachments I use, [but the bare floor one hardly stands up at all without tipping over).]  My biggest issue with this vacuum is the "stretch hose." On the vacuum itself, it is 22" long. When fully extended, it's as long as 65". They want you to detach the floor attachment and use just the handle with stretch hose (with other attachments), OR you can detach the extension wand and use it with a floor attachment (like the dust mop piece in the pictures on QVC, turning the vacuum into a canister vacuum rather than upright).  However, the resistance of the hose is so strong that you can't extend the hose without holding on to the vacuum itself or else the vacuum gets dragged along with you (or tips over at the very least). If I wanted to use the vacuum as a canister vacuum rather than an upright, it'd be nearly impossible to do so since you can't tow the canister along with you. I have the biggest problem while using it on the stairs. I wish I could leave it at the top or the bottom of the stairs and use the handle with the turbo brush, but I can't go more than one step without the vacuum falling over because the resistance is so strong on the hose once you stretch it in the slightest. Instead, I detach the canister from the floor piece and extension wand, and just try to hold it in one hand while using the nozzle handle in my other hand. It's a big pain that I wasn't prepared for with this vacuum.  Overall, I don't think I'd recommend this vacuum just because of the inconveniences I have with it. I'm not very excited that I know I'll be stuck with this vacuum for a least a few years before I can try something else. All of the attachments make you excited about it, but I don't think they make up for the cons. The high ratings really made me think it'd be fantastic, but unfortunately I just don't think it's all that it's worked up to be.  11/7/2012 Edited to Add: I forgot to mention that this thing is a pain to empty. The bin holds very little until it is at is "max full." But if you have any sort of pet hair then it gets all clumped up in the top of the bin instead of the bottom. The bin is shaped so that all the hair/dust/dirt gets caught way up inside it and you have to reach your fingers up to pull it all out. I can't imagine this being possible for anyone with bigger fingers than I have, and my hands are fairly small. Another reason I'm very disappointed with this vacuum. Cannister vacuums are supposed to be easy to empty--just tip the bin upside-down and everything pours out. This vacuum is the opposite of that scenario.

About two years ago I ordered a $28 automatic soap dispenser. For weeks I tried to put up with it but It barely would dispense soap that I eventually threw it out. I had such a bad experience that it has taken me a couple of years before I decided to try this one that came up for review. After trying to get this to work for over 30 minutes, this also went into the garbage as per my experience below.  This is a wall-mounted dispenser. A key is needed to keep the top closed. Two keys, two mounting screws, and 3 extra nozzles come with this dispenser. It requires 4 AA batteries (NOT INCLUDED). The batteries get inserted in the back and there is an ON/OFF switch below the battery compartment. This means it has to be taken off the wall to change the batteries or turn the unit ON or OFF (should one be going away for a while and want it OFF).  I decided to test this before mounting to a wall and lucky I did. After inserting the batteries, I filled it partway, struggled to get the key lock to work to keep the lid closed, turned the unit ON in the back after inserting the batteries, and then engaged the unit. Since air can be in the line, I tried about 30 times to get the soap to dispense and nothing would come out. I even pulled out the nozzle and reinserted it. I had to pull out the nozzle with an open scissor because it was impossible to remove. The green foaming nozzle would not insert into the hole so I just reseated the one that came with it. At one point soap started to leak out of the seam in the back but it would never dispense it through the nozzle.  The instructions are not great but they are still somewhat understandable. After trying for over 30 minutes, I gave up. Besides not dispensing soap, the key lock is difficult to operate and very sticky and there does not seem to be any way to insert the green foaming nozzle if that is desired. The exit hole is just too small. However, these are moot points since after flushing all air from the lines, the soap would not dispense and then started to leak out of the seam in the back. It went right in the garbage.

This SECURA AUTOMATIC COFFEE GRINDER AND SPICE GRINDER (2.5 Ounce Capacity, Silver) works on the same principle as my tried-and-true KitchenAid coffee grinder.  (That grinder has been grinding away for over 20 years--I don’t know why KitchenAid stopped making this great appliance.)  Basically, you put your beans or seeds into the bladed stainless steel bowl, add the plastic lid, add the square plastic cover, and press down on the square cover in 8-second bursts to grind the stuff in the bowl.  An 8-second burst grinds 2 tablespoons of coffee beans to the equivalent of a “percolator” grind.  This is too coarse a grind for my Hamilton Beach Coffeemaker, so my beans need additional 8-second bursts.  Given that my ancient KitchenAid appliance grinds 8 tablespoons of coffee beans to the equivalent of a “drip” grind in just 10 seconds, the Secura grinder won’t be replacing it.  Another reason NOT to embrace the Secura grinder is that the bladed bowl doesn’t hold enough coffee beans at one time for 10-12 cups of coffee.  The “Max Fill” line only accommodates the beans for about 6 cups of coffee.  For 12 cups of coffee, you have to fill the bladed bowl twice, and grind each time in at least two 8-second bursts.  If you don’t grind in short bursts, the grinder will overheat and shut down--and require a 45-minute cooldown.  Finally, the plastic lid that goes onto the bladed stainless steel bowl fits very tightly.  It is quite difficult to remove.  This is a major drawback when you’re opening a container of finely ground coffee beans or seeds.  The grinder bowl, plastic lid, and square plastic cover are all dishwasher safe, but hand washing is recommended.  A reason to follow this recommendation is that plastic becomes cloudy if it’s subjected to high dishwasher temperatures.  I can use this grinder for spices, but I don’t grind a lot of spices, except for nutmeg.  For nutmeg, I use a specialized nutmeg grinder that looks like a peppermill, so that the nutmeg is always freshly ground.

We bought this toaster from Target a while after we moved here, which was two years ago. About a month or two ago stopped wanting to toast at all--as in, when you push down the lever, it doesn't stay down. It acts like it isn't plugged in. It certainly isn't the outlet's problem because we tried plenty of those. So I paid 40 dollars for a toaster that lasted less than two years. If I had the receipt, I could get a replacement or something since there's a two-year warranty, but who the hell keeps a receipt for a toaster for two years? We had a crappy $10 toaster from Walmart before this one, and it lasted probably six years before we upgraded to this [supposedly better] new one (as in, it hadn't even stopped working yet). You'd think a 40 dollar toaster would last a long time and toast bread better than a cheap Walmart toaster. You'd think.  The only reason I'm giving it two stars is because for a year and a half it DID work exactly like it was supposed to. I certainly won't be buying another Black & Decker toaster again!

These MIND READER METAL CANISTERS (3 Piece, Galvanized Metal, Silver) are vastly overpriced (about $21 on Amazon).  They are lightweight, base metal tins with loose-fitting lids.  (A rubber gasket helps seal the lids, but you can’t even lift an empty tin by the lid handle.)  The advertised 6-cup capacity is too little to hold much of anything, unless you want the tins for an employee break room to hold sugar packets, individually-wrapped tea bags, and K-cups or instant coffee packets.  The tins aren’t good for holding other things (e.g., home-baked cookies, hard candies), because of the labels for “Coffee”, “Sugar”, and “Tea”.  Whatever you put in these, it will need frequent replenishment because of the tins’ small size.  Overall, the tins look and feel cheap.  The silver finish has an odd, tacky feel, even when the tins are brand new.  The gold-toned metal labels are hard to read and marginally attractive—yet they are probably the reason for the inflated price.  This set is something that I would expect to find in a Dollar Store.  There are no included “use and care” instructions, so I’m guessing that they are “wipe clean” only.  Dishwasher detergent will probably leave pits in the surface.

Note: There are two models attached to this listing. I am specifically reviewing model 0917.  I will state outright that I do not like electric blankets, after I had one that decided to short out noisily while in use. This is the first electric blanket I have had in many years, and I have some criticisms of it, outside my distrust.  This is not a large blanket at all, but instead seems to be more of a throw size suitable for curling up in a chair or on the couch in front of the television. That’s fine, there’s a use for something like that and if that’s the intended use it’ll be fine. If you want it for a double, queen or king-sized bed, it won’t cover the whole bed, and even that can be ok if you just want coverage for one.  The controls are very straightforward and easy. Power it on, and set the temperature between 1 and 5. It doesn’t require reading a manual or watching a video - the controls are just simple, and that’s the way they should be. Now, that said, it just doesn’t get all that warm. It’s fine for getting the chill off, but if you’re looking for a blanket that will warm you to the core, this one isn’t it. So far the controls haven’t failed, but there have been enough complaints from other reviewers that I’m concerned about the longevity of this blanket.  The blanket itself isn’t great. This blanket resembles a bath throw rug, which is plush and has long strands on the fluffy side. That’s ok, except that this particular blanket sheds. It shed enough that it leaves fluff on your clothes and on whatever furniture on which you’re sitting.  I can’t recommend this blanket. It’s ok, but it lacks the quality at the price point.

I actually purchased two scoops.  This one and the rounder, softer edged aluminum one. The rounder edged one is huge.  And I have smaller popcorn bags, so despite the reviews that reported this one as super sharp edged, I decided to try it...  It arrived in good shape and quickly (I have prime).  It has a "protective film" on the inside that took quite a lot of effort to remove, but I finally prevailed.  It peeled off successfully.  It is drilled on both sides, so you could make it a leftie or a rightie server which is nice. The wholes drilled in it are to help the unpopped kernals to fall through and reduce the number in your served bags.  This one fits nicely into the the smaller bags and loads well.  The edges ARE VERY sharp. But I don't spend time rubbing my fingers down the edges, so I'm ok with that.  It would be nice if they would soften the edges some, thus minus 1 star.  While this is stainless steel, I hand wash it, carefully (sharp edges) because of the handle.  I suppose you could unscrew the handle and throw it in the dishwasher, but eventually you'd probably strip the screw, so I would not recommend that.  All in all, it works very well when you consider handling it properly.  Unless you are allowing very small children to fill the bags, I think it's worth a shot.

I absolutely love this oven. To give you some background, I moved to my new home in Florida last year. With limited resources, I replaced many of the outdated appliances. The one thing I didn't replace was the range/oven. It was a nice gas/electric combo and appeared to be in good shape. Anyway, after using it for about 6 months, I woke up to find the range top working fine (burners) but the inside oven broken. I had to repair guys out here told me it would cost close to $1000 to fix (something to do with needing a mother board). Anyway, long story short, I had been depending on my medium size toaster oven to take the place of the large oven until I can try to replace it. I find it too small for larger things (think an oven roaster chicken). When I saw this Oster, and it was less than $100 (Amazon Warehouse deal), I jumped on it. It is beautiful (stainless steel blends well with my other kitchen appliances), the digital display is clear, large and makes it appear very modern. The size is great, I can fit a chicken in here with no trouble. I will most likely still need to replace my oven (think Thanksgiving), but this will get me through the next 7 months until such time as I can save up for the real thing.

Very cute gnome that's taller and fatter than I expected--and I love it. I almost missed the strings; they come separately (I thought they were already part of the gnome) and they're easy to miss. The gnome came without a shipping box and was delivered in just the product box. Thankfully it wasn't stolen. Inside was the gnome wrapped in just a clear plastic bag. I took the gnome out and tossed the box and bag. Out of the bag, the gnome (branches) were flat against the belly, so I stretched them out. Be very careful! The branches are just glued to the fabric and not very well glued; a couple came out before I realized how delicate I had to be. I couldn't find the lights, so I thought they weren't included. I almost messaged the seller until I thought to check the box again. Nothing was in the box, so I checked the bag again. The bag was weightless, but then I spotted the clear lights. They're super clear and super light, so I missed them the first time. They're easy to wrap around the gnome and take two AAA batteries (not included, mind you).

We wanted privacy but to still let the light in and to be able to easily open and close them. They are perfect for what we needed. They’re also super thin which is perfect for our breakfast nook, so they don’t take up space or get in the way. So simple. Definitely not energy saving, they’re thin, but not see-through.

These stainless steel NORPRO MINI MEASURING SPOONS will make a cute joke gift for a serious cook, but they're not really useful as measuring spoons (tad, dash, pinch, smidgen, drop), because the measures are overly generous.  If you use them with old recipes that call for these amounts, you'll probably overseason your dishes.  My mother taught me that in old recipes, a "tad" is a small amount;  a "dash" is one shake from a shaker (e.g., a pepper or salt shaker); a "pinch" is the amount you can pinch between your finger and thumb; a "smidgen" is a very tiny amount (just enough to achieve the right taste); and a "drop" is one drop of liquid (e.g., a drop from a food coloring bottle).  Of course these are only approximations, but they're used primarily to mean "cautiously season to taste".  In this set, a "tad" is a scant 1/4 teaspoon; a "dash" is a scant 1/8 teaspoon; a "pinch" is 1/16 teaspoon; a "smidgen" is 1/32 teaspoon; and a "drop" is a generous single drop of food coloring liquid.  To me, 1/4 teaspoon is way more than a "tad", and 1/8 teaspoon is much more than a "dash".  My idea of a "pinch" matches up with Norpro's idea of a "smidgen".  In sum, these are NOT the really small measuring spoons that I expected (spoons that would measure amounts of 1/8 teaspoon or less).  They are well made, of solid stainless steel, and they are dishwasher safe.  However, because they measure overly large amounts, I rate them at 2 stars ("I don't like them" on the official Amazon scale).
